data.title: Portland General Electric: A Regulated Utility With A Narrow Moat
data.description:
Article Thesis

2024 and 2025 were two great years for utilities, with short-term tailwinds like lower interest rates and mid-to-long term tailwinds like increased demand for electricity driven especially by data centers.

For 2024, even a passive investment in State Street® Utilities Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLU) would have brought 21.5%, a rare feast for this defensive sector. This is mostly because the market was already positioning itself ahead of the Fed's interest rate easing cycle. For some months, XLU brought even higher returns than S&P 500, and that in the middle of the AI frenzy:

With more focus on certain utilities that benefited disproportionately from the data center demand, like the Southern US ones, we could have easily surpassed the S&P 500.

The positive trend for utilities continued in 2025, but with a lower total return of 14.5%. Besides, I already found most of the Southern US ones slightly overvalued; that’s why I looked mostly for diversified options strategies, because these strategies can still bring high returns even with slight movements in their underlying stocks:

  • A bull Call Spread (long Call option, short lower strike Call) in Evergy (EVRG), which brought ~30.5% return in about three months.
  • A short naked Put in Avista Corporation (AVA), with ~24% return in about five and a half months.
  • A long Call option in Portland General Electric (POR), with ~52% return in about six months.

I will detail in the following the mechanics of this last position and why I see Portland General Electric only as a “Hold” today.

Business Highlights

Portland General Electric, or PGE, is an integrated electric utility company, with operations in the state of Oregon. I like that there is a balanced customer load mix: commercial, industrial, and residential. And the industrial load is also mixed, not just data centers and AI:

Total retail energy deliveries for the nine months ended September 30, 2025 increased 5% YoY, driven by the Industrial segment (of course, with its data centers) with 18% growth. However, the Industrial segment had a much lower contribution in total revenues, only 15%, which means that only about half this growth will translate into revenues.

Like any other regulated utility, PGE enjoys a narrow economic moat due to its natural monopoly. But again, like any other regulated utility, we can’t speak about a strong economic moat, because their returns are capped by regulators. With only one jurisdiction, maybe PGE is slightly riskier than its peers.

Their approved ROE (Return On Equity) of 9.34% is close to the US average, but their realized ROE is lower, at ~8%, with a five-years average of 8.03%. This underperformance is mostly a result of their wholesale operations (buying or selling electricity through long-term and short-term contracts), with energy deliveries down 6% YoY.

Financial Highlights

In Q3’25, PGE reported a $0.94 GAAP EPS, compared to $0.90 one year ago, and a $1 non-GAAP EPS. But much more important is their mid-term outlook. And for a regulated utility, this is quite reliable, because it’s based on their negotiated CapEx and rate base growth:

Let’s not forget, however, their wholesale business segment (10%-15% of total revenues), with a not-so-great performance recently. Therefore, I would model EPS growth closer to the lower end of this range, to be a bit on the conservative side.

Dividend growth should follow closely, because they have a healthy payout ratio of ~65%, with 60%-70% considered healthy for a regulated utility.

Finally, PGE has an investment-grade credit rating from both S&P and Moody’s, BBB+ being in line with other regulated utilities:

Fair Value

For a relative valuation, I look at a comparison with PGE’s five-year averages:

But, as I always said, not all multiples are born equal. For regulated utilities I look at:

  • Price / Book: completely irrelevant for a growth stock, but arguably the most relevant for a regulated utility, because it’s close to Price / Rate Base. We have 8%-9% undervaluation, from 1.33x today compared to 1.45 5Y average.
  • EV/EBITDA FWD: again, very important for regulated utilities because it includes profitability and debt, an essential part of their business model. We have about the same value as the 5Y average.

Looking at the same multiples for the sector, we get about 43% and 28% apparent undervaluation, but this is misleading. We need to combine this with their lower ROE, 17%-18% lower than the sector. Therefore, we get maybe half of that undervaluation or even less.

For an absolute valuation, I use a DDM (Discounted Dividend Model) in two stages:

Div growth

Fair Value

$50.77

With a Fair Value estimate of $50.77, PGE looks about 7% undervalued compared to the current price ($47.46), and this is backed by the relative valuation.

I usually rate a regulated utility as a “Buy” if the undervaluation + dividend yield + dividend growth is greater than 20%. And while we had about 33% back in June, it’s only 7% + 4.4% + 5.2% = 16.6% today, therefore a “Hold”.

Strategy

With a 33% undervaluation + dividend yield + dividend growth in June, PGE was not quite a “Strong Buy” at $40.15, but that was enough for me to initiate a leveraged strategy through an ITM (In-The-Money) Call option: PGE 25 Call expiring Dec-19, 2025, for $15.16 per share, or $1516 per contract.

On November 11, as posted through a comment, with the stock just slightly above $50, I also sold a POR 50 Call Dec’25 for $99. Why did I do that? Because the stock was already around my Fair Value estimate, and I wanted to protect my already big gains from the 25 Call against a pullback. And indeed, the stock retreated a little, to $47.46 today.

The long 25 Call has been exercised just at its expiration, for the dividend from Dec-22, and the short 50 Call expired worthless, which gives us the following results, considering today’s stock price of $47.46:

*This short Call should normally decrease the cost base, since I cashed in a premium, but it was sold very late, so I'll keep modeling the initial cost.

**Please notice that, in a cash-type account, one share would cost $25, therefore above the initial $15.16. That’s why I wouldn’t buy these shares for the dividend, but I would just sell the Call option 2-3 days before its expiration. And this is also what I usually do in a margin-type account, because this would give a very close result.

However, in a margin-type account, I don’t have this constraint of all those $25 per share being blocked, but much less, and therefore the maximum cost is the initial $15.16 per share paid for the Call option. I do look, however, at my total cash balance after buying those shares to be positive, because otherwise I would pay interest. That’s why, with plenty of cash, I sometimes buy those shares for the dividend, for a couple of extra-dollars, although this is not a given, because the stock price usually retreats on the ex-dividend date (and this is why the result is very close to the scenario when I sold my long Call option just before its expiration), so:

  • I closed half-position at $47.46.
  • I am hoping for a further comeback in the next 2-3 days.

Risks

Since I am already closing my position, I don’t want to speak too much about risks, but I do want to explain the reward/risk for that bull Call position. This deep ITM Call option has about the same leverage in both directions, ~2.6X. Therefore, the risk on the downside is substantial.

But let’s see a bit more: what is the main risk for PGE with the stock at $40.15, a 5.2% dividend yield and a 5%+ dividend growth. Yes, sure, there is this drag from their wholesale business, but I already incorporated that by modelling only 5.1% dividend growth, very close to their lower edge.

The main risk, and more difficult to quantify, is wildfire risk. However, back in June, there were only about 2-3 months left with this risk, and PGE also has a solid wildfire risk management:

In conclusion, I saw this risk with a very low probability in comparison with the possible reward. PGE had ~23% undervaluation and an abnormally higher dividend for a regulated utility, considering its estimated growth and an interest rate easing cycle, which made the reward/risk for this leveraged position appear very favorable.

Takeaway

Portland General Electric is an integrated electric utility company, with operations in the state of Oregon. PGE's regulated utility model offers a narrow economic moat, but underperforming wholesale operations (10%-15% of revenues) and over-reliance on industrial customers (1/3 of energy deliveries) increase the uncertainty.

A DDM derived Fair Value, and backed by relative valuation, is $50.77, suggesting PGE is about 7% undervalued at $47.46, with a healthy 4.4% yield and a healthy ~65% payout ratio. PGE is rated 'Hold' as current undervaluation plus dividend yield and growth total only 16.6%, below my 20% buy threshold.

A long POR 25 Call Dec '25 initiated in June, complemented with a short POR 50 Call Dec'25 in November, delivered ~58% ROI in ~6 months, but with valuation normalization and moderate growth, new positions are less compelling.

data.title: Rent the Runway: Back In Growth Mode, But Profitability Remains The Key Question
data.description:
Rent the Runway (RENT) returned to growth in its rental revenue through new strategies and inventory expansion, resulting in a customer base that surpasses its 2019 level. However, costs are increasing faster than revenues, and margins compressed in the most recent quarter, which puts the sustainability of this growth into question unless costs are brought under control.

The Company

RENT gives access to designer clothes at a fraction of the price. It offers three types of services: subscription, reservation (or one-off rental), and resale. In the subscription model, customers subscribe and receive access to five to ten different items of clothing for a wide range of situations, from workwear to loungewear, casual, maternity, to accessories, jewelry, handbags, and other items. Customers also have the option to keep the items for longer if they like them, or return them and select other clothes. The reserve model is a one-off rental with a period of 4 to 8 days. On the resale model, customers have the option to buy the used clothes at a discount, which is a category that is growing substantially in revenue, and does not require a subscription.

The company acquires products through wholesale, share by RTR (Rent the Runway), and exclusive designs. The wholesale segment represents about 30% of product acquisition, where the company buys from brands in volume and at discounted prices. In the Share by RTR segment, the company pays nothing upfront, and the brands receive a portion of the net profits over time. However, over time, the brands can end up receiving more than if RENT were just to buy the piece, and this segment represents about 48% of product acquisition. The company also partners with brands to manufacture exclusive designs based on its customers preferences and to have more durable pieces. Exclusive designs account for about 22% of the company’s product acquisition.

The business model divides consumers' opinions. On one hand, some love the variety and the thrill of wearing something new every so often. On the other hand, others see it as a waste of money, arguing that they would rather spend that same amount on pieces they can keep. Still, the model is definitely a conversation starter, which leads to a more organic growth for the company.

Recent developments

In the Q3 2025 results, RENT converted more than 50% of its debt into equity, invested in inventory, created an affiliated program to drive organic growth, improved its homepage algorithm, leading to a 70% increase in click-through rates, and increased transparency with a clearer add-on pricing experience. These initiatives have not only improved liquidity but have also demonstrated higher conversion rates. In my view, these developments are very positive and position the company for continued organic growth in the coming quarters.

Financials

Looking at the company’s history, the business suffered in the 2020 pandemic period, losing about 58% of its customer base, from more than 133 thousand in 2019 to about 55 thousand in 2020. However, the pandemic period is in the past now, and in the last quarterly release, the company reported almost 149 thousand customers, above pre-pandemic levels, which is also a 12% YoY increase in the customer base from the quarter. Subscription and Reserve rental revenue increased 16% YoY, while revenue from the sale of items, which is the other revenue on the income statement, increased 10% YoY. This revenue from the sale of items is on a strong upward trend, more than doubling since 2022. A side note here: I think this is funny, because in some of the videos I watched of consumers sharing their experience with the company, they felt the most excited not by having as many options to rent, but by seeing a cheap sale price for an item they liked; they were very inclined to buy it, and this relates to what is happening in the company’s revenues.

On the cost side, total costs and expenses increased 15.8% YoY, from $89.6 million in Q3 2024 to $103.8 million in Q3 2025. Most of this cost increase was from rental product depreciation and revenue share (payments for the brands), which increased 33.6% YoY, from $28.2 million to $37.7 million. When comparing this $37.7 million expense with the $32.9 million depreciation charge on the income statement, which decreased from a figure of $35.5 million a year earlier, it becomes clear that the company is paying more to the brands than before, while depreciation costs have remained nearly the same. This highlights a less favorable cost structure for the company, which is a negative point for investors to keep an eye on. On the positive side, SG&A increased 7% YoY, from $21.2 million to $22.7 million, which is less than the total revenue increase in percentage terms. This resulted in an operating loss of $16.2 million, lower than the previous year's $13.7 million operational loss. The company also had a one-time gain of $96.3 million, which was from the debt restructuring. This turned a would-be net loss of $19.8 million into a net income of $76.5 million. On the balance sheet, the company is losing money, but the debt situation has improved. Cash decreased YoY from $77.4 million to $50.7 million, while total debt decreased from $373.7 million in Q3 2024 to $203.1 million in Q3 2025.

Competitors

RENT’s main competitor is Nuuly. RENT stands out for its luxury designer event outfits, while Nuuly focuses on everyday style variety with greater size inclusivity. Nuuly is the market leader despite starting later, in 2019, and it has surpassed RENT's customer base in just a few years. Nuuly is owned by Urban Outfitters (URBN), which allows it to source inventory at cost from URBN's sister brands, but it also has an enormous variety of brands available for consumers to choose from. The strategy of catering to a more everyday style is working better for Nuuly since it charges less than RENT, and Nuuly’s subscriber count is growing much faster. The recent changes in RENT's strategy are promising, but the inventory increase cited by RENT's management seems to also be targeting a more everyday style (according to internet comments), which is not really RENT's competitive advantage. As things stand today, it seems that Nuuly may continue to have the advantage over RENT due to a lower cost to the consumer and a broader target audience, while RENT still needs to prove it can be profitable.

Valuation

I think the best metric to use with RENT is the EV/Sales, as it accounts for the company's capital structure, including debt. The company is trading at 1.42x EV/Sales while the sector median is 1.38x. So, not much apparent upside there. The company is also trading at 3.74x P/E GAAP (TTM) while the sector median is 20.25x. However, that is due to the on-off revenue from debt restructuring, making the company reach a positive net income in the quarter, but this revenue will not show up in the next quarter, and the company may continue to have negative earnings. RENT is also trading at a Price/Sales of 0.12x, versus the sector median of 0.95x, which is very cheap, but in my view, it will only matter if the company successfully reduces costs, or manages to increase revenues faster than costs.

Risks

The fashion industry is competitive, fragmented, and rapidly changing. Shifting consumer preferences and trends or a shift away from subscription models in favor of ownership or other alternatives could reduce the customer base. The company’s specific model could also be replicated by the very brands it partners with. Consumer retention is also a challenge; the company must keep the clothes feeling like new as they pass from consumer to consumer. For example, if the company keeps a piece in circulation for too long, it could make it feel worn or dated, disappointing customers and harming the company’s reputation. Conversely, if the company renews its stock too often, the costs would be unsustainable, so, it’s a hard balance, and executing this wrong could hurt profitability.

Conclusion

While the company is moving forward with renewed strategies, reaching more clients, and seeing the results in revenue increases, the cost side is increasing faster. I think the best move is to wait and see if the company can grow revenues faster than costs, which is why I view the stock as a “Hold”.

data.title: John Hancock Fundamental Large Cap Core Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • U.S. stocks rallied strongly during the third quarter of 2025, ending on a high note that offset some short-lived sell-offs and spikes of volatility throughout the period.
  • Within the broad-based S&P 500 Index (SP500), the IT, communication services, and consumer discretionary sectors notched the biggest gains, while the consumer staples and real estate sectors were the biggest laggards.
  • Stock picks in the IT, energy, and healthcare sectors hampered performance versus the S&P 500 Index during the quarter.

Market review and outlook

U.S. stocks rallied strongly during the third quarter of 2025, ending on a high note that offset some short-lived sell-offs and spikes of volatility throughout the period. Softer-than-expected economic data, especially in the labor market, and the first interest rate cut of the year in September—thanks to an increasingly more dovish U.S. Federal Reserve—helped fuel investors' appetite for equities. Corporate earnings strength also provided markets with confidence that the economic backdrop could support further stock gains. A wave of AI deals announced in September was another driving force. Within the broad-based benchmark, the IT, communication services, and consumer discretionary sectors notched the biggest gains, while the consumer staples and real estate sectors were the biggest laggards.

Contributors and detractors

Stock picks in the IT, energy, and healthcare sectors hampered performance versus the benchmark in Q3. The biggest individual detractor was a lack of exposure to NVIDIA Corp., the dominant player in the AI chip market. A non-index stake in liquefied natural gas company Cheniere Energy (LNG) also detracted as investors sold energy stocks on the back of abnormally low oil prices. Elsewhere, a non-index position in brewer and spirits supplier Anheuser-Busch (BUD) also detracted. The company's share price sank after reporting financial results that fell short of investors' expectations due to weakening demand for its products in the United States and falling sales volumes in key international markets.

Conversely, security selection in the industrials sector helped the strategy's relative performance. Additional boosts came from an overweight position in the communication services sector, as well as an underweight position in financials. Top individual contributors to relative performance included an overweight position in Alphabet (GOOGL, GOOG), which benefited from earnings that exceeded expectations. The company was also boosted in September by news that it avoided harsh antitrust penalties in the United States. Another standout was U.S.-based equipment rental company United Rentals (URI), whose shares rallied on the back of solid revenue results and expectations that the company would benefit from the government's One Big Beautiful Bill Act, which gives companies more financial incentives to build infrastructure. Also of note was a non-index position in Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM), which is expected to benefit from continued strong demand for chips with AI applications.

Portfolio changes

Portfolio changes in Q3 included eliminated holdings in healthcare, real estate, IT, and communication services. We directed proceeds to new holdings in the consumer discretionary, communication services, IT, and healthcare sectors. The team sees opportunity across the style spectrum and seeks to remain relatively balanced. We're currently seeing more opportunities and marginal tilts toward value.

data.title: AZZ: A Structural Valuation Disconnect For This Infrastructure Company
data.description:
Following the Q2 2026 results of AZZ Inc. (AZZ), I believe that the company makes an attractive buy opportunity. There is a clear disconnection between the two segments of the company, and this is why the valuation is attractive. The precoat metals segment is not in very good shape because of cyclical headwinds related to its consumer-facing nature and the weak HVAC and appliance market, but at the same time, the Metal Coatings segment is surfing on a strong tailwind of infrastructure demand that should keep being sustained. I believe that the market is seeing AZZ as a standard steel processor, while it is more of an infrastructure service provider with high margins, accessible at a now low valuation “thanks” to the headwinds of the precoat metals segment.

This, combined with the now reduced leverage at 1.7x and a business model close to how a “toll road” would operate that shields AZZ from the exposure to steel price volatility (but not volumes). I like the asymmetric risk profile of AZZ: the precoat segment does face cyclical headwinds with a potential pressure on its margins that could drop below 20% EBITDA if the consumer demand turns out to be weaker than expected, but this is offset by the Metal Coatings segment and the strong underlying infrastructure demand it is benefiting from. The company is well protected against price volatility thanks to index-linked customer contracts to other commodities such as zinc and natural gas, making it a play against commodity swings.

A Structural Valuation Disconnect

As mentioned, I believe that the case for AZZ relies on its undervaluation. It is indeed consistent with the cyclical weakness of the Precoat Metals segment that peers in the infrastructure and galvanizing are not seeing, but considering the quality of the company and my conviction on its recovery, the fact that the market is pricing AZZ to be a standard metal processor while it actually is an infrastructure service provider is an opportunity.

Below you will find a comparison of AZZ’s valuations with two of its peers: Valmont Industries (VMI) and Worthington Enterprises (WOR), the first being its primary peer given their similar focus in utility structures and galvanizing sectors, and the second being representative of value-added metal processing companies exposed to consumer and construction cycles.

As you can see, the Forward EV/EBITDA of AZZ positions it between its two peers, but a lot closer to WOR than it is to VMI: I think that it should be the other way around. The EBITDA margin of the Metal Coatings segment above 30% should justify an increase of its multiple as the Precoat segment starts to stabilize. In addition, the heavy discount relative to the P/CF ratio dispute the strong profitability of the company and its deleveraging momentum is a clear signal of undervaluation to me. For the next Q3 2026 earnings, the decisive element will be how well the Precoat Metals segment holds up. For now, I would say that AZZ would be better valued with a minimum P/CF ratio of 10x, implying a 50%+ upside. However, this target will only be reached after the Precoat Metals segment stabilizes, and this could take a few quarters, but cash flows should remain the key focus for a “service business”.

In opposition to steel companies with activity related to commodity prices, AZZ has a business model that is closer to how a “toll road” would work. Basically, AZZ has no ownership on the steel that it coats. AZZ is billing for a service, and this is the same fee no matter what the price of steel is. The service is billed relative to the surface or the weight, and relative to peers such as Commercial Metals Company or Worthington, this is a competitive advantage: AZZ doesn’t depend on prices or inventory write-downs.

Q2 2026 Earnings And Forward

Recent results are highlighting the ongoing divergence within AZZ’s operations. Overall, the sales reached $417.3 Mn, which is a small (but positive) 2.0% YoY growth. This was also slightly below the consensus, but this is not what really matters for now. The quality of the income statement increased: the adjusted diluted earnings per share reached $1.55 (a 13.1% growth), which goes to show that the cost discipline is strong, and that the lack of spectacular growth is offset by better margins, which the market is not really considering in the valuation. I think this is a mistake. Factoring in one offs and equity income, the GAAP EPS saw a 150% increase, which can seem impressive, but I don’t think that you should pay attention to GAAP earnings with AZZ.

However, the important thing to do here is to look at the performance of each segment of the company. First, the metal coatings segment, in a very good shape, is the growth driver of the company. Sales reached $190.0 Mn: a 10.8% YoY growth driven by the strong demand in infrastructure, highways, bridge, and transmission markets. This is a very high margin activity with an adjusted EBITDA margin of 30.8%. I expect this strong dynamic to keep being sustained as the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act (IIJA) directly drives steel volumes up: galvanizing and coatings.

Now, the other segment of the company is not in a very good shape. The sales of the precoat metals segment decreased to reach $227.3 Mn (down 4.3%), due to the poor market dynamics in end markets (HVAC, appliances, and residential construction), suffering from the sensitivity to interest rates and the negative consumer sentiment. But, I don’t think that we should be overly negative about this segment, as the adjusted EBITDA margin is still 20.2% (down 0.90 percentage points), which is strong with regard to the drop in volume and highlights a strong cost discipline. The fact that an EBITDA margin above 20% is kept when the cycle is negative should give us hope for a rebound, because this market dynamic is not going to last forever. With these results, AZZ is showing that they are resilient and disciplined. Right now, as investors, what we need to do is to take advantage of the situation to find a great entry point before the valuation goes back up, and I think that the timing is appropriate now.

Looking ahead, as Q3 results are soon coming, here is what you should expect. The winter weather is usually coming with weaker volumes in construction and galvanizing. This is going to put the Precoat segment’s margin under pressure, so monitoring the cost discipline of the company and how the margin is going to hold in YoY will be crucial. A strong signal would be to see the margins of the segment kept above 20%, and this would confirm the buy rating.

In addition, this quarter is going to be the first quarter with the new repricing of the Term Loan B of August, which should offset the seasonality effect with reduced interest spreads directly improving cash flows. Overall, the strength of cash flows is also crucial to monitor, as at the center of the case for AZZ stands the discounted P/CF ratio and its deleveraging momentum.

An Improving Balance Sheet

Another reason to like AZZ is the fact that the company has a strong cash generation, used to deleverage the company following the precoat metals acquisition. Looking at the balance sheet here clearly shows that the debt is going down, with a net leverage now at 1.7x. I do think that this is a very strong feature, as a source of risk is the decreasing momentum of the precoat metals segment could raise questions about the future potential of cash flow generation and the sustainability of the debt: AZZ is doing exactly what they need to do in their situation: leveraging synergies, improving margins overall, and cutting down the debt.

In addition, the lower level of leverage is significantly changing the risk profile of the company that was once a levered turnaround stock, now positioned to be a compounder, but still valued like it were not. The interest expenses are going down, mechanically because of the debt reduction, but also thanks to a cut following the repricing of the Term Loan B that is further strengthening the cash generation potential.

Optimistic about the success of its strategy and the increasing quality of the balance sheet, the dividend was raised this year to a now 0.74 FWD yield. While the dividend is definitely not the appeal of AZZ, it is nice to see that the approach to the distribution is conservative but stable, and this gives me reassurance about the quality of management.

As the company is crystalizing synergies and deleveraging, the door is open to further expansion through potential M&A opportunities since the galvanizing market is a fragmented one.

Risks

The key risk that could put my buy rating at risk is if the current dynamic of the consumer economy lasts for too long, dragging the precoat metals segment further down because of residential construction and appliances. In addition, a negative dynamic in the interest rates environment would further hurt the market and probably bring the EBITDA margin below the 20% mark. From there, everything will hinge on the metal coatings segment to keep the company alive, while for now the negative dynamic is only a short time headwind allowing us to benefit from a low valuation.

Another risk to have in mind with a company like AZZ is that while we are immune to the volatility in steel prices, this doesn’t mean that the steel market is not affecting AZZ. Notably, the availability of steel is a crucial point. Issues with the supply chain or with further tariffs could reduce volumes, and mechanically suppress the demand for AZZ services.

Finally, I will mention the operational risk. AZZ is proving to be a resilient company with a strong cost discipline, and this is why I am confident that they are going to navigate this difficult environment efficiently and come back from it in a good position, adverse news about operations could put the buy rating at risk.

Conclusion

I think that the divergence between the two segments of AZZ has created a significant discount on the markets and that, given the quality of the company, it should trade at higher levels. AZZ is a high-quality compounder addressing the needs of the infrastructure market, and the weakness of its precoat metals segment is only cyclical, while the market is pricing it as a long term structural drag. What AZZ really is a high margin service provider, with a business model close to how a toll road operator would work. The balance sheet is healthier than ever and the Metal Coatings segment is showing durable and impressive growth. I do think that AZZ comes with an asymmetric risk-reward profile with a high long term upside potential. The key here is to be patient.

data.title: Microsoft Positioned For Strong Growth
data.description:
Microsoft (MSFT) is one of the largest tech companies, with a more than $3.5 trillion market cap. The company is incredibly well positioned with artificial intelligence with its OpenAI stake and the growth of Azure. As we'll see throughout this article, we expect substantial growth from the company that will generate substantial shareholder returns.

Microsoft Financial Performance

Microsoft had strong financial performance with impressive double-digit growth across the board.

Microsoft saw 18% revenue growth (17% CC) driven by the company's continued cloud performance. At current growth rates, the cloud division is roughly a year away from being Microsoft's largest, with total company revenue for the quarter approaching $77.7 billion. The core software nature of the company's business means Microsoft maintained strong margins.

The company had a 49% operating income percentage, leading to $38 billion in operating income for the company. The company managed to grow its GAAP margin by 2%, which is why it managed an incredibly impressive 24% growth in operating income. Adjusting for other expenses, Microsoft earned $27.7 billion in net income, or $3.72 / share in GAAP earnings.

Overall, that puts Microsoft at a P/E of ~33x.

Microsoft Financial Returns

The specifics of Microsoft turning that into shareholder returns is below.

Microsoft returned $10.7 billion to shareholders in the quarter, showing its lofty valuation with its total yield at just over 1%. For example, the company's dividend is a mere 0.76%. At the same time, Microsoft has massively ramped up capital expenditures for growing Azure demand, with $34.9 billion in capex for the quarter.

Microsoft has guided for continued capex growth, but at least the company is seeing revenue tied back to Azure. Moreover, the structure of data center leases puts the companies less on the hook. Microsoft saw $25.7 billion in FCF, an increase supported by continued top line growth along with leases minimizing the impact of the AI wave.

To us, it's key that the company doesn't overbuild for artificial intelligence demand. We do see some risks that Microsoft, unlike Amazon and Google, does not have nearly as strong of a custom silicon offering versus Trainium and Ironwood TPUs. Microsoft could see demand shift away from Azure if it can't effectively compete with internal silicon over the long run.

Microsoft has recently announced its next generation CPU, but on the GPU front all is quiet.

Microsoft Segment Performance

Microsoft has benefited from substantial strength within its cloud business and continued growth.

The company saw 112% commercial bookings growth driven by demand from OpenAI. Analysts have discussed to death the concern around OpenAI and whether the company will be able to fulfill what is now beyond $1 trillion in capital obligations. We see it as incredibly unlikely, which presents a major risk factor to these bookings and the work that's being done on them.

Microsoft said at least through the next 3 quarters of the fiscal year, the company is capacity constrained. It's spending roughly $75 billion / year on GPUs and CPUs, and Azure specifically saw ~39% YoY growth. Last quarter, Microsoft finally revealed Azure's annual revenue by itself versus a business unit at $75 billion for the year.

1:1 tying Azure revenue growth to OpenAI is difficult, however, as part of the company deal, OpenAI is committing $250 billion in additional revenue along with secured GPT model rights until 2032.

Still, the company has managed to continue impressively growing its revenue, and the division has more than $49 billion in quarterly revenue. Doing that with continued strong gross margins and revenue growth highlights Microsoft's core strength. We expect this division of Microsoft to continue its rapid growth.

The company's Productivity and Business division saw continued growth driven by Microsoft 365 commercial cloud. Smaller growth in the cost of revenue enabled strong gross margin improvements, which resulted in even faster growth in the company's operating incomes. The ability to invest in AI and future growth while growing margins shows the massive scale of the company's business.

The company's slowest-growing divisions are its Windows OEM and Gaming divisions. The company's Windows OEM and Devices revenue grew 6%, supported by growth of 18% in OEM with demand ahead of Windows 10 end of support. However, this is more of a one-time transition from Windows 10, and long-term demand remains more questionable.

Microsoft originally called Windows 10 the last version of Windows, so what Windows 11 does remains to be seen.

Microsoft Future

Microsoft's future comes from three key strengths in our view.

(1) Antitrust

First, Microsoft went through its antitrust pain in the late-90s. The company managed to handle this situation, which means, unlike Google or other companies facing major antitrust trials, it has a lot more breathing room. That has enabled Microsoft to make much larger acquisitions in recent years.

(2) Diversified Portfolio

As a part of the above, Microsoft has an incredibly impressive and diversified portfolio. It owns assets ranging from Windows to Azure to its massive ChatGPT stake to GitHub to LinkedIn to Xbox and Blizzard. That diversified portfolio makes the company dramatically less susceptible to market changes.

Among the concerns with OpenAI for Microsoft's future growth was OpenAI's non-profit organization. With OpenAI restructuring as a corporation, the non-profit arm will own a 26% stake in the company, second to Microsoft with a 27% stake.

With OpenAI targeting a $830 billion valuation, Microsoft's stake is at $225 billion with that planned capital raise. The two largest AI companies are Anthropic and OpenAI, and Microsoft recently signed a deal with Anthropic. Microsoft is well positioned to lead in AI, though it has major competition.

(3) Valuation

The last piece is valuation. Microsoft is at $16.5 in non-GAAP EPS annualized based on the last quarter, or a P/E of ~30x. That's with double-digit earnings growth YoY across the company's diversified asset portfolio. That's a low valuation with the company's double-digit growth and it's a valuation the company can comfortably grow into.

Google is the closest peer to Microsoft. Google is trading at a 27x P/E ratio. Google has leading models through Gemini that are competitive with Microsoft's ChatGPT OpenAI rights. However, the biggest reason why we see Microsoft as more valuable is the lack of threat.

Microsoft's business model is not threatened the same way that Google search is and Microsoft has much more diversification in its portfolio. At the same time, it is still incredibly well positioned. Overall, that makes Microsoft a valuable investment.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is Microsoft's valuation. Microsoft has a more than $3.5 trillion valuation, versus roughly $100 billion in annual FCF. That's a 3% FCF yield, which means the company needs future growth to justify its valuation. Continued success by Microsoft is key for Microsoft's ability to drive future shareholder returns.

The second largest risk, in our view, is Google. Google is similar to Microsoft with a leading cloud platform and a leading stake in AI through its high-performing Gemini models and partnership with Anthropic. Google owns roughly 14% of Anthropic. More importantly, Google also has leading custom silicon through TPUs, ahead of Microsoft's custom silicon.

Conclusion

Microsoft is one of the largest companies in the world, with a $3.6 trillion market, but, despite that, we still feel that it has more room to grow. That's because the company has an incredibly impressive portfolio of diversified assets, which gives it much less risk in a changing world versus traditional tech companies that have suffered.

The company is trading at a P/E of ~30x on its most recent earnings with impressive double-digit growth. Microsoft is continuing to drive substantial shareholder returns with its recent, largest $60 billion share repurchase program. More importantly, the company is the center of the AI wave with its Azure + ChatGPT and recent Anthropic partnership.

Overall, that makes Microsoft a valuable long-term investment.

data.title: Moderna: On Transition From Pandemic Windfall To Multi-Product Platform, Initiating At Neutral
data.description:
Executive summary

I initiate Moderna (MRNA) with a hold rating with a target price of $25, which is under the current trading stock price. I believe Moderna remains one of the most advanced global mRNA platforms, with three approved commercial vaccines, a broad pipeline, and meaningful cash reserves. I view the current risk-reward as balanced but not compelling enough to initiate a position.

Near-term commercial execution in the respiratory vaccine is improving. Cost discipline seems to be ahead of the plan according to the recent earnings; however, I see structural uncertainties across COVID demand durability, pricing dynamics, and regulatory timelines (namely, Robert Francis Kennedy and Trump risk) for upcoming launches, and the inherently long gestation nature of the oncology and rare disease pipeline development and commercial success.

Moderna's key asset overview

Of note, the three approved commercial products are Spikevax (the original COVID vaccine), mNEXSPIKE (an updated COVID vaccine), and mRESVIA (an RSV Vaccine). The company is guiding that there are 30+ developmental candidates across respiratory, latent viruses, oncology, and rare metabolic diseases, with 37 active clinical studies.

  1. The COVID franchise (Spikevax and mNEXSPIKE) remains Moderna's largest contributor (95%+) and accounts for $971M in Q3 2025 product sales. Although the 2025 US vaccination season is tracking within expectations (~26 million doses), the company is projecting a 20-40% decline in cumulative retail vaccinations in 2025. This decline is likely driven by weaker public enthusiasm, payer pressure, and non-annual boosters amongst younger patients. COVID-19 vaccines are no longer a "must-have" vaccine, and demand is increasingly a winner-take-all in the >65 age segment, with minimal uptake in patients under 50. I think the key issue is that COVID demand remains structurally unpredictable, influenced by variant evolution, payor policies, and public willingness to vaccinate annually. I do not see a clear return to pandemic-level volumes with long-term steady-state demand below the current consensus.
  2. RSV Vaccine (mRESVIA): mRESVIA is approved in 40 countries for adults 60 and above, and in 31 countries for high-risk adults 18-59. The launch began in Q3 2024, with $4M 9M2025 sales (a very early launch print, and I believe investors shouldn't read too much from this). There are competitors like Pfizer (Abrysvo), GSK (Arexvy), and Moderna, which entered as a third large player. I see RSV as a multi-billion dollar market, but payor management, seasonal dynamics, and unclear product differentiation (limited comparative real-world effectiveness data - but some advantage around reactogenicity, strong safety data from Moderna's COVID safety history, and convenient one-shot immunization) remain key questions. As such, I see mRESVIA contributing meaningfully to mRNA's valuation (potentially surpassing COVID sales by 2027-2028 with COVID franchise sales further abating), but not at a scale achieved by the early incumbents.

  3. Upcoming respiratory (Flu) launches (key 2026 catalysts): Flu (mRNA-1010) is filing across the US, EU, Canada, and Australia. The ph3 immunogenicity sub-analyses have been presented at ESWI (data showing non-inferiority and some incremental relative efficacy improvement in the high risk subgroups, albeit the superiority over standard quadrivalent vaccine remains unproven), potentially ushering Moderna into the $10B+ global flu vaccine market dominated by egg-based vaccines. For commercial success, I believe incremental improvements in vaccine efficacy vs. the standard of care are needed to gain needle-moving market share. Flu + COVID combination (mRNA 1083) may potentially get approved in the EU and Canada in 2026. EMA review is currently ongoing, US refiling is pending FDA guidance. I think the commercial rationale here is strong, as it offers two-to-one convenience, but regulatory complexity is greater, and US regulators have asked for additional clarity. I believe the current political climate is not ideal, given the current administration and Robert Francis Kennedy's anti-vaccine stance. Net-net, I assign a moderate probability to 2026 approval in ex-US, with a combination vaccine providing a substantial medium-term value driver for the stock but also carrying material regulatory risk.

Pipeline overview: some interesting assets on the horizon, but without risk

Although I think Moderna's pipeline is broad and well diversified, it's not without meaningful execution risk. For example, Moderna's CMV franchise failed to meet the primary endpoint in phase 3, and Moderna discontinued the congenital indication, which represented a major setback considering the CMV franchise was one of Moderna's highest peak sales opportunities.

Key pipeline assets deep dive

Norovirus (mRNA-1403) vaccine program: the phase 3's insufficient case accrual has delayed the readout. As such, additional enrollment for the 2025 and 2026 seasons would be needed. One risk I see is that case-driven studies historically carry a greater degree of timing uncertainty.

Rare disease portfolio: MMA (mRNA-3705) is fully enrolled, with positive cumulative data published at ICIEM, and propionic Acidemia (mRNA-3927) registrational trials are currently ongoing. Of note, the company previously explored other metabolic indications (i.e., PKU, GSD1a in preclinical stages years earlier), however, the current clinical franchise centers around MMA and PA, both are life-threatening organic acidemias driven by single-enzyme deficiencies. The two programs utilize lipid nanoparticle (LNP) formulation and are delivered intravenously through repeat dosing, mRNA-encoded replacement enzyme therapy. This is Moderna's first non-vaccine therapeutic to reach registration studies. This interests me personally, as mRNA therapeutics for metabolic disease can offer high biological plausibility. Still, I believe the key pushback from investors would be questions about durability and long-term safety, which remain unproven for chronic, multi-dose therapy.

Oncology programs: Here, I believe oncology assets are the most significant long-term value driver for Moderna. The oncology franchise has clear blockbuster potential. However, I believe the regulatory pathway for personalized mRNA therapies is complex, and furthermore, the scalability of manufacturing for individualized neoantigen therapy remains unproven at commercial volumes at the moment. Furthermore, I see event-driven trials may create significant uncertainty around timelines. Net-net, I see the oncology pipeline as a long-dated call option for speculative investors.

  • Intismeran (mRNA-4157) is a personalized cancer vaccine that Moderna has partnered with Merck on previously. The asset is fully enrolled for a phase 3 adjuvant melanoma study. The two phase 3 NSCLC adjuvant studies are enrolling (with/without neoadjuvant treatment), and additional programs in RCC, bladder cancer, and first-line metastatic settings are currently ongoing. I believe the proof-of-concept from the 2023 KEYNOTE-942 update demonstrated promising RFS and OS trends. The current phase 3 trials are event-driven, and I expect that readouts are likely in 2026-2027.
  • mRNA-4359: are T-cell engagers. The phase 1b data publication (from ESMO 2025) showed an early sign of activity with acceptable safety. The two phase-2 cohorts (i.e., melanoma and metastatic NSCLC) are currently enrolling.

Financials: highly COVID-driven

From a high-level revenue perspective, Q3 2025 revenue was $1Bn, which is down 45% YoY, and 9M 2025 revenue was $1.26B, down from $2.270B in 9M 2024. Importantly, 95%+ of the sales are driven by the COVID-19 franchise, which shows how concentrated Moderna's portfolio is. I believe the decline reflects the post-pandemic normalization of COVID demand and the delayed launch of new products. The company has guided $1.6Bn-2Bn, which I believe is consistent with improving seasonality (but leaving notable Q4 dependence).

In terms of cash position, Moderna holds $6.6Bn in cash and investments as of September 2025, down from $7.5Bn in Q2 2025, amid massive operational cash burn of $2.8Bn in 9M 2025. Using a similar cash burn or a decline in cash burn driven by the company's concerted efforts to reduce OPEX, I estimate the cash runway to around 2027-2028, even assuming the absence of a dramatic COVID collapse.

Furthermore, Moderna and Blackstone Flu's funding structure (low-single-digit royalties and up to $750 milestones) may be deemed another indicator of high risk, as some "bears" may construe it as Moderna preferring to offload financial risk or as indirect evidence that the flu program is high risk and capital-intensive. On the contrary, I believe the off-balance sheet financing is a very smart approach for a company like Moderna at the moment. It offers a "market validation" around the approvability of the product and the product's commercial potential, as I believe established funds like Blackstone Lifescience tend to do extensive due diligence before making investments.

Valuation - target price of $25

I value Moderna at a $25 target price at a 10% WACC, given its commercial nature and a modest 2% terminal growth rate, and the breadth of its pipeline assets. While Moderna trades at a discount to peak COVID era valuation, I believe the stock is fairly priced at the current levels based on stabilizing, but structurally smaller COVID base business, modest RSV contribution, and pipeline optionality with significant but distant oncology upside, substantial cash buffer but continued near-term losses expected, and execution risk across multiple parallel launches.

Risks

The key risks are a) COVID demand continues to decline faster than expected, considering the 95% revenue concentration in COVID as of 2025, b) regulatory delays are a possibility, especially for the flu, combo vaccines, and certain rare disease programs, where I believe the FDA may request additional studies (due to case accrual delays, and disagreements on surrogate endpoints), c) cost cuts may prove insufficient, despite $2.1Bn cost improvement over the past 4 quarters. Furthermore, I believe the spending could creep upward with oncology scaling or COGS elevation, leading to the 2028 breakeven thesis slipping. Lastly, I believe competitive risks remain material, especially with competitors such as flu (GSK, and Sanofi), RSV (GSK, Pfizer), COVID (Novavax, Pfizer/BioNTech), Oncology (BMS, Merck, checkpoint inhibitors, and cell therapies).

Conclusions: initiating with a hold rating with a $25 target price

I initiate coverage of Moderna with a hold rating, given a neutral risk-reward profile. The key positives are a) strong mRNA-based technology, a large pipeline, a strong cash position, improving cost discipline, and substantial optionality in oncology and rare diseases. Negative aspects are: limited near-term revenue visibility, especially with the high degree of reliance on the COVID-19 pipeline, pipeline complexity, uncertain regulatory timelines, and long-dated R&D payoffs. Net net, I see Moderna's equity story offers optionality, not certainty. While I believe Moderna will remain a central player in mRNA-based medicine, the shares already reflect the company's medium-term potential while appropriately discounting long-dated, uncertain oncology value. I remain on the sidelines until I get high clarity on a) respiratory franchise growth trajectory (2026 launches), b) phase 3 oncology outcomes (which I believe will be the most important valuation drivers), and c) operational progress toward 2028 cash breakeven.

data.title: Meta: The Company Benefiting From AI Right Now
data.description:
Introduction

Meta (META)(META:CA)(ZMET:CA) needs no introduction, as the majority of the people on Earth have used apps derived from the company’s umbrella. But as it is my first analysis of the company on Seeking Alpha, I’ll take a broad view of the operation and see what the current dynamics are for the company to see if it has a place in my portfolio and yours as well.

Meta has transformed itself since the COVID pandemic, and it has now completely (or nearly) pivoted its long-term vision towards Generative AI and Wearable AR, away from the infamous Metaverse

Current Dynamics

I’ll first start with the most recent earnings to grasp the current financials of the company. Meta reported a mixed set of earnings, which initially made the company shed nearly $220B in market cap. This was mainly due to a $16B one-time income tax charge, which caused an 83% drop in GAAP net income. That’s why there was a significant miss of $5.66, resulting in an EPS of $1.05. Though on an adjusted basis, net income stood at $18.64B, which is the true performance of the company and makes the business way healthier than first expected. Revenue stood at $51.24B, which is a beat of $1.83B and a massive 26.2% Y/Y increase.

Digging deeper, Meta’s ad business has continued to perform extremely well, thanks to improvements in engagement and monetization, apparently due to the firm’s increased use of AI. Thanks to these improvements, advertisers are willing to pay even more for them, and that’s how Meta has been able to increase the average price per ad by around 10%. More so, the company has noted that the AI-powered recommendation models led to an acceleration in user engagement, and thus consumers are staying longer on Instagram and Facebook. Though outside of the ad business, Meta raised some eyebrows with its CapEx numbers, as the company is spending money like never before, around $19.4B in the quarter, with almost all of it going towards Nvidia H200/B200 chips to build massive data centers. The narrative has been the same at Google, being that the firm prefers to overinvest in AI rather than underinvest, as apparently it would be way more detrimental to the company.

Overall, I believe that this was a relatively good quarter for Meta, especially as we can see the real-life implications of AI directly improving the ad business, which is the big revenue driver.

Outside of the most recent earnings, Meta has made the right choice to pull back from the original vision of the Metaverse, which was an initial, ill-fated endeavor, in my opinion. The company will cut around 30% of the budget for Fiscal 2026 as the division has now lost a staggering $73B, since the launch in 2021. The burn rate is finally being capped as the company goes fully towards Gen AI and wearable hardware. At the same time that VR headsets are struggling, Meta’s smart glasses are actually a genuine hit with customers, pushing the narrative of spatial AI instead of the Metaverse.

Llama 4 has also been the key reason why Meta has been dominating its AI space and changed its fundamental architecture as it moved to a Mixture-of-Experts design. It has a native multimodality that was trained on text, images, and videos altogether, allowing for an interleaved dataset. The commercialization strategy is also rather different than competitors, as it was released as an “open-weight” model, which gives it away and ensures that developers around the world build on their architecture. And this, unlike the Metaverse, is generating money for the company directly, especially with the Advantage+ integration. I’m also particularly bullish on "Project Avocado" which could be a closed-source, high-end model mainly for enterprise clients. Keeping Llama “Open” to win over developers and having Avocado win over large enterprises could be a true game-changer for the company.

Valuation

I would say that right now, Meta is relatively cheap, all things considered, with its P/E Non-GAAP standing at the lowest among its peers at 22.84. That’s a far cry compared to Apple at 36.64 and Microsoft at 33.58, which have lower growth and lower profitability. And despite the increased CapEx for the company, the boosts from AI are directly creating cash, which leaves Meta with the same level of FCF as previously, despite the massive investments. Though the company’s FCF yield is relatively low at 2.7% at the current valuation. Now the real value behind Meta would be its family of apps that are incredible intangible assets, thanks to the amount of customer data and the ensuing network effect that makes Meta the largest and most dominant social media platform in the world.

I also believe that Meta has some of the best growth prospects for the +$1T market cap companies available on the market. It has already posted some of the strongest growth over the past 3 years, with its revenue growing by a staggering 17.06% CAGR over the past 3 years and Net Income by 26.62% CAGR. I expect the company to keep the same kind of growth as it keeps bettering its AI-integration into its ad segment, further improving ad impressions and thus the price the company can charge. Furthermore, Meta has also now unlocked monetization for WhatsApp after sitting idle for many years, with the “Click-to-WhatsApp” ads that are seeing extreme effectiveness, as well as Business messaging fees and AI agents. Meta has also captured the emerging market for smart eyewear, easily beating Apple and Google thanks to the very smart partnership with Ray-Ban that saw a growth of 200% Y/Y. I expect the company to continue to grow its revenue by 15% CAGR through 2030, thanks to all of these monetization opportunities.

Meta is seeing some of the best margins in the industry, thanks to its size and family of apps, with its gross margin of 82.01% and its net margin of 30.89%, which is simply incredible. And yes, there was the tax hit, but Meta basically used a superb quarter to absorb a massive future tax liability related to the “One Big Beautiful Bill” act. With Meta having strong Ad pricing power, I expect margins to continue to improve as it remains the core of Meta’s revenue, more so Meta’s AI ad suite, Advantage+ now has a run rate of over $60B annually, which is a higher margin segment directly boosting profitability for the company. Meta also has superb capital efficiency metrics with a ROE of 31.1% and a ROCE of 30.7%.

Risks

There is the question of the insane level of CapEx, with Meta projected to continue its high spending to build its Titan clusters. The argument is that yes, AI is boosting ad revenue now, but the risk is that Meta is overbuilding and will incur high depreciation costs in the future that could be harmful for the company, especially if there is a deceleration in revenue.

Meta is also facing renewed regulatory pressure from the European Commission as it is being investigated for its measures against competitors in the AI space. Although Meta prevailed against the FTC's efforts to force the company to spin off WhatsApp and Instagram in the US, the renewed front on the European side could prove to be more damaging for the future of the business. Basically, the suit accuses Meta of gatekeeping the messaging world to give itself an unfair advantage in AI.

Conclusion

I believe that right now, with the stock declining due to the poorly perceived quarter of the company, it is the right entry point to buy shares in Meta. It has some of the best prospects ahead with a relative valuation that makes it the cheapest of the Mag 7. By shedding the initial Metaverse goals and focusing on what is accretive right now, I believe that Meta is now a Strong Buy.

data.title: XSD: Why AI-Name Chip Stocks Aren't Done Yet
data.description:
Much ink has been spilled regarding the “broadening out” of the stock market in recent months. Yes, cyclical areas like Financials, Industrials, and even Consumer Discretionary are at all-time highs, but semiconductor stocks remain a favorite when stretching out the timeframe.

Today, I’m revisiting the State Street SPDR S&P Semiconductor ETF (XSD), a non-cap-weighted chip ETF that has outperformed in the second half of the year and since the April lows. I had a buy rating on the fund back in July. Up 26% since then (total return), XSD has beaten the S&P 500 by 15 percentage points. With a decent valuation and chart, I reiterate a buy rating.

XSD with YTD Alpha

According to the issuer, XSD seeks to track the modified equal-weighted S&P Semiconductor Select Industry Index, which provides the potential for unconcentrated industry exposure across large-, mid-, and small-cap stocks. It is a tactical fund that is used to express industry views on semi stocks within the Information Technology sector.

XSD is a small-sized ETF with $1.6 billion in assets under management as of December 24, 2025. That’s up from $1.3 billion at the time of my previous analysis. The annual expense ratio is low at 35 basis points, while the trailing 12-month dividend yield is low at 0.25%.

Share-price momentum has been stellar for the better part of the past 8 months, earning the product a pristine A+ ETF Grade in that category by Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. But chip stocks are notoriously cyclical, with volatility also coming from ebbs and flows in the AI megatrend. Hence, XSD’s Risk Grade is a poor D-.

Finally, on liquidity, XSD is decently tradeable—average daily volume is not high (just 56,000 shares), but the median 30-day bid/ask spread is not very high at 13 basis points. Still, using limit orders around the market open is probably a prudent move.

For an update on the portfolio, the 3-star, Bronze-rated ETF by Morningstar has mixed exposure across the style box. Being a modified-cap-weighted allocation, no single stock will command a large weight. Moreover, a majority of the portfolio is invested mainly in US SMID caps. The international weighting in the aggregate is 8.8%.

On valuation, the price-to-earnings ratio is actually a bit lower compared to my July assessment. At 25.1x earnings, it’s no bargain, but the long-term EPS growth rate is high at 15.9%. The resulting PEG ratio is a modest 1.58x.

XSD: Portfolio & Factor Profiles

XSD is a 100% Information Technology product. Focused in the Semiconductor industry, the top 10 positions account for a somewhat low 29.3%. So, single-name volatility is not terribly high.

What’s more, the chip earnings season is still several weeks away, so I expect its overall volatility to be in check through the first few weeks of the new year.

XSD: Holdings & Dividend Information

Speaking of seasonal trends, December through March has historically been a mildly bullish stretch. Volatility has occasionally struck in April, however.

XSD: Calendar Tailwinds

The Technical Take

With high momentum and a still-reasonable valuation, XSD’s technical situation is generally solid. Notice in the chart below that shares remain above their long-term and rising 200-day moving average—that tells me that the bulls control the primary trend. The chips ETF reached technical overbought conditions during the later innings of the post-Liberation Day rally, and now a consolidation phase is ongoing.

While there has been some relative weakness to the broader global stock market since October, this is really an “innocent until proven guilty” chart. Long-term support is down in the $270s (which is where the 200 dma comes into view, too), while a large amount of volume by price begins at the July 2024 and January 2025 peaks. Resistance is in the $348 to $359 zone.

XSD: Consolidation Within an Uptrend, Rising 200dma

The Bottom Line

I have a buy rating on XSD. The S&P Semiconductor ETF is chopping around within a broader uptrend. The valuation is attractive, and I expect another year of gains ahead.

data.title: Virtus Newfleet Multi-Sector Bond ETF Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fixed Income Market Review

The third quarter of 2025 was marked by shifts in macroeconomic, geopolitical, fundamental, and supply and demand dynamics. Markets rebounded from the lows following the “Liberation Day” announcements as immediate economic impacts remained subdued. The final outcomes for tariffs and other policies remain unknown, as do their longer-term economic impacts. However, financial markets gained confidence over the quarter that the economy remained solid, inflation remained rangebound, and geopolitical risks showed early signs of easing.

Economic data in the U.S. continued to point to a resilient economy. Growth improved after a weak first half, with inflation staying contained and unemployment holding near historically low levels despite some downside surprises. This allowed the Federal Reserve (Fed) to resume easing policy with a 25-basis-point (BPS) reduction at the September Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) meeting. The Fed has acknowledged that while elements of its mandate are in conflict, it has chosen to focus on protecting the labor market in the near term. Global monetary policy remains desynchronized, with divergences among the European Central Bank, the Bank of England, and the Bank of Japan. Geopolitical developments were mixed during the third quarter, with volatility re-emerging in the Russia/Ukraine conflict following the Alaska summit in August, while the Middle East saw some signs of progress.

The continuing market rebound resulted in generally positive financial market performance in the quarter. Fixed income sector performance was also positive, with interest rates lower during the period. Most spread sectors outperformed U.S. Treasuries, and the U.S. Treasury curve steepened slightly. The 2-year Treasury yield decreased by 11 bps, the 5-year Treasury yield decreased by 6 bps, the 10-year Treasury yield decreased by 8 bps, and the 30-year Treasury yield moved 4 bps lower.

Fund Performance Review

The Fund (Class I) returned 2.59% in the third quarter versus the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Index (LBUSTRUU) return of 2.03%.

Positive Contributors

  • U.S. Treasury Securities: The Fund's underweight to U.S. Treasuries had a positive impact, as most spread sectors outperformed U.S. Treasuries and had positive excess returns.
  • Emerging Market (EM) Sovereigns: The Fund's overweight and selection in the EM sovereign sector were positive.
  • High Yield (HY) Corporates: Allocation and selection within the sector were positive.
  • Investment Grade (IG) Corporates Selection: Issue selection versus the benchmark was positive for the period.

Performance Detractors

  • Residential Mortgage-Backed Securities (RMBS): Allocation to RMBS over agency mortgage-backed securities (MBS) and selection within RMBS had a negative impact on performance.
  • Bank Loans Selection: Issue selection was negative.
  • IG Corporates: The Fund's underweight versus the benchmark was negative for the period.

Current Fund Strategy and Positioning

  • Increased exposure to MBS, EM sovereign bonds, EM local currency bonds, and HY corporates.
  • Reduced exposure to bank loans, U.S. Treasuries, RMBS, and cash.
  • In addition to changes to the Fund's sector allocation during the quarter, we continue to optimize positioning within sectors based on our view of the best relative value.

EM Debt and Non-U.S. Exposure: The J.P. Morgan EMBI Global Diversified Index (JPEIGLBL) returned 4.75% in 3Q, with Em Hy outperforming Em Ig. EM corporate debt returned 3.18%. In 3Q, the U.S. imposed a 50% tariff on Brazilian exports, straining ties and pushing Brazil toward China. Former president Jair Bolsonaro was convicted for attempting a coup after his 2022 loss. In Argentina, President Javier Milei's party suffered a major defeat to Peronists in Buenos Aires, sparking market volatility. Later, the U.S. pledged $20 billion in aid to support Milei's reforms. In Ecuador, President Noboa ended diesel subsidies, triggering widespread protests as fuel prices surged. The Fund's overall EM exposure was higher throughout the quarter. The Fund maintained a preference for U.S. dollar assets and Em Hy over Em Ig.

IG Corporates: IG corporate spreads ended 3Q in the low 70s—the lowest levels since 1998—while yields moved lower by 18 bps to 4.81%. Despite lower spreads and yields, IG saw positive fund flows in every week of 3Q. Institutional buyers remain active as well. Though supply for the quarter was heavier than the five-year average, it was easily absorbed. Upgrades outpaced downgrades year-to-date, though at a slower pace than in recent years. BBBs are at their lowest percentage of the overall market in a decade. We are focused on defensive industries given tight valuations, with a preference for utilities, pipelines, and capital goods.

HY Corporates: The HY market, as represented by the Bloomberg High Yield 2% Issuer Capped Bond Index (LF98TRUU), gained 2.54% in 3Q. Credit spreads tightened by 23 bps to +267 bps. Stronger-than-expected economic growth, a dovish Fed pivot that produced a 25-bps rate cut in September, resilient corporate earnings, and significant inflows contributed to performance. Media outperformed due to anticipated deregulation and positive merger and acquisition developments. Chemicals and packaging underperformed due to weak global demand.

Securitized: The securitized markets had positive total and excess returns for 3Q. Spreads tightened despite elevated issuance across all securitized sub-sectors as investors sought out additional income and spread within securitized sectors. We added agency MBS during the quarter as we felt the sector had lagged alternatives such as the IG corporate sector. While our strategy continues to focus on up-in-quality given signs of stress on the margin for certain borrowers, we're also selectively exploring individual transactions that present promising yield opportunities lower in the capital stack.

The "net asset value" (NAV) of the Fund is determined at the close of each business day, and represents the dollar value of one share of the Fund; it is calculated by taking the total assets of the Fund, subtracting total liabilities, and dividing by the total number of shares outstanding. The NAV of the Fund is not necessarily the same as its intraday trading value. Fund investors should not expect to buy or sell shares at NAV because shares of ETFs such as the Fund are bought and sold at market price (not NAV) and are not individually redeemed from the Fund. Thus, shares may trade at a premium or discount to their NAV in the secondary market. Brokerage commissions will reduce returns. NAV returns are calculated using the Fund's daily 4:00 pm NAV, and include the reinvestment of all dividends and other distributions (reinvested at the Fund's NAV on distribution ex-date). Market price returns are calculated using the 4:00 pm midpoint between the bid and offer, and include the reinvestment of all dividends and other distributions (reinvested at the 4:00 pm bid/offer midpoint on distribution ex-date). Market price returns do not represent the return you would receive if you traded at other times.

Index: The Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index measures the U.S. investment grade fixed rate bond market. The index is calculated using a float-adjusted, capitalization-weighted methodology on a total-return basis.

The Bloomberg U.S. High-Yield 2% Issuer Capped Bond Index is a market capitalization-weighted index that measures fixed rate non-investment grade debt securities of U.S. and non-U.S. corporations. IOPV, or Indicative Optimized Portfolio Value, is a calculation disseminated by the stock exchange that approximates the Fund's NAV every fifteen seconds throughout the trading day.

data.title: The Gabelli Global Financial Services Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Dear Fellow Shareholders,

For the quarter ended September 30, 2025, the net asset value (“NAV”) per the Class I Share of The Gabelli Global Financial Services Fund (“the Fund”) increased by 8.7%, compared to a 5.5% increase for the MSCI World Financials Index (MSWFI). The common stocks of global banks and other financial institutions performed generally well as financial markets were robust and loan credit quality remained mostly healthy. Second quarter (“2Q”) financial results for most of the Fund’s holdings were strong, and many continued their multi-year share repurchase plans and / or raised their dividends. The Fund initiated three new positions, which are discussed below. At quarter end, the Fund’s aggregate holdings were valued at approximately 1.0 times book value, 1.1 times tangible book value (“TBV”) and 11 times expected 2025 earnings per share (“EPS”).

The common stock of Commerzbank (3.7% of net assets as of September 30, 2025; up 20% in the quarter) was the largest positive contributor. The company reported a 48% increase in 2Q net earnings before restructuring charges and raised its full year 2025 guidance owing an improved outlook for net interest income (“NII”). The company also announced a EUR 1 billion share repurchase plan. The Bank of New York Mellon’s common stock (3.3%; +20%) was the second largest contributor. The company reported a 27% increase in 2Q EPS driven by a 13% increase in assets under custody to $55.8 trillion. Additionally, it repurchased 1.4% of its shares. Finally, the common stock of Standard Chartered (2.8%; +17%) also benefited from robust earnings (68% increase in underlying 2Q EPS) and share repurchases (2.3% share count reduction in 2Q).

The largest detractor was First Citizens BancShares (4.3%; -8%) whose common stock fell modestly owing to the shift in the Federal Reserve's rate outlook towards easing, including a 25 basis point cut in September. A reduction in the federal funds rate usually results in lower NII and earnings in the short term for First Citizens owing to its asset sensitive balance sheet. The second largest detractor was the common stock of Daimler Truck (DTRUY) (1.6%; -13%), a leading global truck manufacturer with significant financial services operations. Daimler reported a 61% decline in 2Q EPS as economic softness and tariff uncertainty negatively impacted demand for trucks, particularly in the U.S. Nevertheless, Daimler appears to be well positioned to weather current industry weakness and participate in the eventual recovery, given its strong financial position with about EUR 6 billion in net industrial cash and investments (22% of its market cap) and market leading position in North America.

Top Ten Selected Holdings*

• Commerzbank AG (CRZBY) 3.7
• Bank of New York Mellon Corp. 3.3
• Cavco Industries Inc. 3.0
• Capital One Financial Corp. 2.9
• Standard Chartered plc 2.8
• First American Financial Corp. 2.7
• Trustco Bank Corp. NY (TRST) 2.5
*Percent of net assets as of September 30, 2025.

Let's Talk Stocks

Aspen Insurance Holdings Ltd. (1.7% of net assets as of September 30, 2025) (AHL - $36.71 - NYSE) is a Bermuda-based property & casualty (P&C) insurer. The company was purchased by affiliates of Apollo Global Management (APO) in 2019 and completed an IPO for approximately 18% of its shares in May of 2025 at $30 per share. Aspen's financial results have improved markedly in recent years with its combined ratio (losses and expenses divided by premiums) below 90% since 2023. On August 27, 2025, the company entered into a definitive agreement to be acquired by Sompo Holdings, a leading global P&C insurer based in Japan, for $37.50 per share in cash. This price equates to about 1.3 times TBV. The Fund initiated its position after the transaction was announced at an attractive discount to the merger price, considering the high likelihood that the transaction will close as scheduled in the second quarter of 2026.

Bank of East Asia Ltd. (2.1%) (23 HK - $1.55/HK$12.10 - HKEX) is a leading Hong Kong-based financial services group. The company has one of the largest retail banking networks in Hong Kong with 46 branches and significant operations in China with a focus on the Greater Bay Area. Although the Bank of East Asia's recent financial results have been negatively impacted by elevated credit costs related to commercial real estate in Hong Kong and China, it has maintained a robust capital position with a 23.7% Tier One Common Equity ("CET 1") ratio. Executive Chairman David Li, and his sons, co-CEOs Adrian and Brian Li, own about 6% of the outstanding shares and other Li family members own approximately another 3%, providing significant alignment of interests with shareholders. The common stock trades at only 7.2 times EPS for the latest 12 months ("LTM"), 31% of TBV and offers a 5.7% dividend yield.

Old Republic International Corp. (1.7%) (ORI - $42.47 - NYSE) is a Chicago-based specialty insurer focused on P&C and title insurance. The company has an impressive long-term track record of prudent underwriting with book value per share having compounded at a 12.7% annual rate, including dividends, over the last 57 years. Its investment portfolio is very conservative with extremely limited exposure to high risk or illiquid securities, such as limited partnerships, derivatives, hedge funds or private equity investments. The common stock trades at 12.1 times Ltm EPS, 1.7 times TBV and has a 2.7% dividend yield. The company's recent earnings are somewhat depressed owing to industry-wide weakness in title insurance because of the depressed U.S. housing market. Old Republic has paid a cash dividend in each of the last 84 years and raised it for 44 consecutive years. Insiders own approximately 7.6% of the outstanding shares.
